headlines.title: GRAVEL NEEDED FOR LAKE WORTH TEST
headlines.subtitle: Chemists Unable to Complete Examination of Foundation at Dam.
headlines.fullText: Chemimis testing the cement in the dam at Lake Worth reported to. headlines.title: WRECK PROBE ENDS ACCIDENT THEORY BACKED BY FACTS
headlines.fullText: County officers have completed their Investigations of the Frisco wreck Bear Trinity Park Sunday morning, Jen. 11, when three members of the train crew were killed, and it is probable there will be no indictments in connection with the case. J. C. Thompson, teamster living on the Hove Foundry road West of ": "aparecen charging murder on the following Tuesday and since then has been undrr district 11,950 grand bond, jury but the has Sixty-seventh not indicted him. Thompson was alleged to have placed a white mare on the bridge wo he could Billed wy missing train. The wreck killed by a lausing min win the since been learned. however, the white mare probably was not on the bridge, but on the track at the south end of the bridge. It has HET un determined the tender of the engine left the track before it reached the bridge, indicating there in where it struck the mare. headlines.title: YEARLY REPORT SHOWS Y.W.C.A. SCOPE LARGER
headlines.subtitle: 27,000 Girls Reached During 1913 and 7,700 Received Help.
headlines.fullText: The tremendous scope of Young Women's Christian Association work in Fort Worth was shown at the annual meeting Thursday night. Mrs. J. H. samed that a total of 27,095 girls were reached through the association in 1912. During 1912, 7,700 girls, women and children were helped by the travelers' aids. These were strangers of every character and nationality. Mrs. Smith reported that 765 girls were reached through the extension department. This work, under Miss Opal Ray, extension secretary, is carried on among the women employes of the large industrial punta, Through the educational and religious departments of the association st year 200 ales girls were reached through the social department: 170 girls were furnished Wathomem a 60 girls were boarded in the humand Fifty five thousand lunchen The officers of the new board directors elected at the annual meeting will be chosen Feb. 12 at associa- ginning the financial campaign was postponed from Friday until Monday at 9 a. m. new members of the board of directors are Mmes, Ed Williams, W. I. Watkins, William Reeves, J. C. Reed, L. B. Bishop, A. K. Want, M. B. Head. E. Baldridge, B. C. Evans, and John F. Lyons. Members of the board re-elected are: Mmes, W. F. Barnum, George Armstrong, W. M. Short, W. R. Sawyer. W. D. Reynolds, Q. T. Moreland and E. L. Huffman. A committee consisting of Mrs. Geo. Reynolds, Mrs. W. R. Thompson and Mrs. W. R. Sawyer will nominate the new officers of the board.

headlines.title: 8 ROOMS TO BE ADDED TO NORTH SIDE SCHOOL
headlines.subtitle: Patrons Block Plan to Move High Classes to Another Building.
headlines.fullText: An eight-room addition will be built to the North Side High School. Plans Muller Pollard architects, and bids for the construction of the addition will be invited immediately. Dengite action will be taken at the next regular meeting of the school board. The school board has had two plans under contemplation for the Improvement of school conditions on the North Side. One plan was to make the present high school building strictly a grammar school, and to move the high school to the Circle Park school building making it exclusively a high school. The present high school is used jointly for high school and grammar school classes. The board believed that by ading an auditorium and rooms for the manual training department to the Circle Park building the system could be benefited. Adjoining the Circle Park building is a six-acre city and the board figured that this park would give ample play ground for the students, supplying baseball and football grounds and grounds for other sports. A meeting of North Side citizens and the school board was held Thursday night in the North Side high school auditorium to discuss the changes. More than 200 citizens protested against the changes contemplated and recommended the plan of adding to the present high school building. T. A. Altman, member of the school board, presided. Introducing the speakers. Superintendent Je Wellcant- board "penne com sidered by the board. Friday morfi- ink. George Clarke, chairman of the Building and found" committe* ma webes building will be adapted au board. Rooms for the manual training department and two class rooms will be added.
